topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the coffee shop felt heavy, pressing down on my chest until it was hard to draw a full breath. I stared down at the lukewarm latte, the foam a pale, mocking swirl. When Liam finally turned to me, his smile was bright, genuine, and utterly devoid of recognition. "Hey," he said, his voice warm as always, "you look like you're thinking about something serious." I managed a tight nod, my throat suddenly dry. The air around him seemed to vibrate with an easy familiarity that I was no longer part of. Every casual gesture—the way he leaned back, the easy laugh that crinkled the corners of his eyes—felt like a spotlight shining on a ghost. I gripped my mug so hard my knuckles turned white, wishing I could melt into the worn wooden table.

[story 2]

I walked through the park, the autumn leaves crunching under my feet with an amplified, grating sound. Each rustle seemed to echo the hollowness inside me. I watched couples walk hand-in-hand, their shared glances speaking a language I was excluded from. When Liam spotted me near the fountain, his pace quickened, a sudden burst of energy that left me feeling sluggish and disconnected. "Wow, look who it is!" he exclaimed, stopping abruptly. He extended a hand, his grip firm and friendly. I hesitated, my gaze dropping to the pavement. I offered a weak smile, the muscles in my face feeling stiff and unresponsive. "Hey," I mumbled, the sound barely escaping. He squeezed my hand, a familiar pressure, but it didn't reach me. His eyes scanned my face, a flicker of confusion crossing his features, before he smoothly transitioned to talking about his weekend plans, leaving me standing there, a statue carved from quiet disappointment.

[story 3]

The apartment was too quiet. I paced the length of the living room three times, the worn rug feeling abrasive beneath my bare feet. I kept catching my reflection in the darkened window, studying the lines etched around my eyes, searching for a flicker of connection, any sign that the memory of shared history still existed for him. When Liam finally arrived, he dropped his keys onto the counter with a clatter. "Sorry I'm late," he apologized, running a hand through his hair. He looked at me, a momentary pause in his routine, and then his expression smoothed over into something neutral, almost polite. "What's up?" he asked, his tone suggesting I was interrupting something important, not someone he used to share secrets with. I felt a sudden, sharp tightening in my chest, a physical clench that made my stomach turn. I just stood by the doorway, unable to summon the words to explain the sheer weight of what I was feeling.
